title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption:
The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.

This line chart tracks the gender wage ratio in the United States from 1980 to 2010 across three distinct income percentiles: the tenth, fiftieth, and ninetieth. The vertical axis measures the ratio as a percentage, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis marks five-year intervals. In 1980, all three lines begin in the lower portion of the chart, between 60% and 65%. The line representing the tenth percentile starts near 65%, rises steadily to approximately 75% by 1990, and continues a gradual ascent to finish just above 80% in 2010. The fiftieth percentile line begins slightly lower, around 62%, but climbs more steeply over the three decades, crossing the other lines to end as the highest value on the chart, slightly above 80% in 2010. The ninetieth percentile line starts near 62%, rises to roughly 73% by 1990, and then flattens significantly, remaining nearly horizontal through 2010 at approximately 74%. By the end of the period, the fiftieth and tenth percentile lines converge near the top of the visible range, while the ninetieth percentile line sits distinctly lower than the other two. The visual trend shows an overall increase in the ratio for all groups, with the most pronounced growth occurring between 1980 and 1990, followed by a period of slower change or stagnation depending on the specific percentile.
